content.text: Writing a good prompt by hand is iteration-intensive and brittle: it couples task performance to a human's intuition rather than to evidence. Automatic Prompt Optimization — systematized in the
framework — replaces that hand-crafting loop with a compiler. You describe what you want through typed
; the framework's optimizer ("Teleprompter") then automatically generates instructions and selects few-shot demonstrations that maximize a metric on your data.
DSPy (Declarative Self-improving Language Programs) is a Python framework released by Stanford NLP that treats language model pipelines as
rather than as collections of hand-written string templates. The key insight is separating three concerns that manual prompt engineering conflates:
— the sequence of reasoning steps, expressed as a Python program.
— Signature declarations that name fields and describe their intent.
— the actual instructions and examples placed in the context window, generated automatically by the optimizer.

Automatic Prompt Optimization is the step where the third concern is resolved without human involvement. DSPy's optimizers — called Teleprompters — take your program, a metric function, and a labeled training set, then search over possible instructions and demonstration pools to find a configuration that maximizes metric score. The resulting "compiled" program is identical in structure to the original; only the text of the prompts has been updated.

Manual prompt engineering is not a robust engineering practice. It relies on the practitioner's intuition about what the current model version responds to, it degrades silently when the model is updated, it is impossible to parallelize, and it cannot scale to pipelines with dozens of LLM calls. Empirically, a carefully hand-crafted prompt often underperforms an automatically optimized one even when the human author has domain expertise.

Automatic optimization matters because it shifts prompt quality from a one-time human effort to a reproducible optimization process. This means that when you switch models, change your training data, or discover a new failure mode, you can re-run the optimizer rather than starting over with intuition. It also enables multi-stage pipelines where each stage is independently tuned — something impractical to do by hand.

In the agentic AI context specifically, automatic optimization is the foundation for agents that improve themselves. An agent's decision-making loop can be framed as a DSPy program, and the optimizer improves every sub-step's instructions in one sweep.
: Optimization is deterministic given the same data and model, making it trackable via source control like any other artifact.
: Program logic and prompt content evolve independently — changing your reasoning pipeline does not require re-crafting prompts from scratch.
: Each module in a multi-hop chain is optimized, so gains multiply rather than average across stages.
: Re-compiling the same program against a different LLM (e.g., switching from GPT-4 to Claude 3.5) adapts prompt style automatically.
: BootstrapFewShot generates demonstrations from your own training data, eliminating the need to hand-write examples.
: The optimizer directly maximizes your evaluation function, so what improves in optimization is exactly what you measure in production.
: You must define a metric function before optimization can begin. A metric that is easy to write (e.g., exact string match) may not capture the nuance you actually care about.
: Running MIPROv2 or BootstrapFewShot with a large teacher model over a meaningful training set spends many LLM calls. This is a one-time cost, but it can be significant.
: Reliable optimization needs at least 50–200 labeled training examples. With fewer examples, the optimizer may overfit to the training set.
: The generated instructions may be verbose or include counter-intuitive phrasings that are hard to audit manually.
: DSPy programs require the dspy library at runtime. Exporting to a raw prompt string is possible but loses the ability to re-optimize later.
DSPy's compilation model proceeds in three conceptual phases:
. You write a Python class that inherits from
, declares Signatures (typed input/output schemas with field descriptions), and wires them together with Modules like
. At this point the prompts are blank stubs — only the structure exists.

. The Teleprompter runs trials: it draws candidate few-shot demonstrations from the training set (for BootstrapFewShot) or proposes candidate instruction strings (for MIPROv2/OPRO) and scores each candidate using your metric on a development split.
) and loaded for inference. Its Predict modules now carry the winning instruction text and a curated set of demonstrations in their context, so every call to
DSPy ships several Teleprompter implementations with different search strategies:

The diagram below shows the compilation pipeline. Your program definition and training set flow into the Teleprompter, which runs an iterative search loop guided by the metric function. The output is an optimized program that carries auto-generated instructions and curated demonstrations.
Automatic prompt optimization with DSPy is a good fit when all of the following are true:
You can write or collect at least 50 labeled training examples (input → expected output or label).
You have a quantifiable metric: exact match, F1, ROUGE, or even an LLM-as-judge call with a rubric.
The task involves multiple LLM calls in sequence (multi-hop QA, RAG pipeline, agent loops) — the optimizer's gains compound across steps.
You anticipate re-running optimization as data grows or models change — the upfront cost amortizes over many inference calls.
You want to switch underlying LLMs without re-writing prompts — compile once per model.

Avoid it when: your task has no defensible metric, you have fewer than ~20 examples, you need sub-100ms latency at optimization time, or the prompts must be human-readable and manually auditable in every detail.
to point at OpenAI, Anthropic, a local Ollama instance, or any LiteLLM-compatible provider.
: one class per I/O schema, with field descriptions that carry semantic intent for the optimizer.
objects with at minimum an input field and the ground-truth output.
: instantiate a Teleprompter class and call
— it returns a new program object with optimized prompts.
and run it against your held-out test set to confirm improvement.
Example 1: Minimal Sentiment Classifier with BootstrapFewShot

import dspy
from dspy.teleprompt import BootstrapFewShot

# 1. Configure LLM backend
lm = dspy.LM("openai/gpt-4o-mini", temperature=0.0)
dspy.configure(lm=lm)

# 2. Define a Signature — typed I/O contract with field descriptions
class SentimentSig(dspy.Signature):
    """Classify the sentiment of a product review."""
    review: str = dspy.InputField(desc="A product review written by a customer")
    sentiment: str = dspy.OutputField(
        desc="Exactly one of: positive, negative, neutral"
    )

# 3. Build a simple Module
class SentimentClassifier(dspy.Module):
    def __init__(self):
        self.classify = dspy.Predict(SentimentSig)

    def forward(self, review: str) -> dspy.Prediction:
        return self.classify(review=review)

# 4. Prepare training data
trainset = [
    dspy.Example(review="Amazing product, totally worth it!", sentiment="positive").with_inputs("review"),
    dspy.Example(review="Broke after two days, very disappointed.", sentiment="negative").with_inputs("review"),
    dspy.Example(review="It does the job, nothing special.", sentiment="neutral").with_inputs("review"),
    dspy.Example(review="Exceeded every expectation, love it.", sentiment="positive").with_inputs("review"),
    dspy.Example(review="Complete waste of money.", sentiment="negative").with_inputs("review"),
]

# 5. Define a metric
def accuracy_metric(example, prediction, trace=None):
    return prediction.sentiment.strip().lower() == example.sentiment.lower()

# 6. Compile with BootstrapFewShot
teleprompter = BootstrapFewShot(metric=accuracy_metric, max_bootstrapped_demos=3)
optimized = teleprompter.compile(SentimentClassifier(), trainset=trainset)

# 7. Use the optimized program
result = optimized(review="Works great but shipping took forever.")
print(result.sentiment)   # e.g., "neutral"

# 8. Save for later
optimized.save("sentiment_classifier.json")
MIPROv2 optimizes both the instruction text and the few-shot demonstrations, giving a larger search space and typically higher scores for complex tasks.

import dspy
from dspy.teleprompt import MIPROv2

lm = dspy.LM("openai/gpt-4o", temperature=0.0)
dspy.configure(lm=lm)

# Multi-step reasoning pipeline: retrieve context then answer
class RetrievedQA(dspy.Module):
    def __init__(self, num_passages: int = 3):
        self.retrieve = dspy.Retrieve(k=num_passages)
        self.reason = dspy.ChainOfThought("context, question -> answer")

    def forward(self, question: str) -> dspy.Prediction:
        passages = self.retrieve(question).passages
        context = "\n".join(passages)
        return self.reason(context=context, question=question)

# Assume trainset is a list of dspy.Example with question, answer fields
# trainset = load_hotpotqa_trainset()  # your data loading code here

def exact_match(example, pred, trace=None):
    return pred.answer.strip().lower() == example.answer.strip().lower()

# MIPROv2 parameters
optimizer = MIPROv2(
    metric=exact_match,
    auto="light",          # budget: "light", "medium", or "heavy"
    num_threads=8,         # parallel trial evaluation
    verbose=True,
)

# compiled_qa = optimizer.compile(
#     RetrievedQA(),
#     trainset=trainset[:200],
#     valset=trainset[200:250],
# )
# compiled_qa.save("hotpotqa_miprov2.json")

# Load and run inference
# qa = RetrievedQA()
# qa.load("hotpotqa_miprov2.json")
# print(qa(question="Which element has atomic number 79?").answer)

import dspy

class JudgeSig(dspy.Signature):
    """Evaluate answer quality."""
    question: str = dspy.InputField()
    gold_answer: str = dspy.InputField()
    predicted_answer: str = dspy.InputField()
    score: float = dspy.OutputField(
        desc="Score from 0.0 to 1.0 — 1.0 means fully correct and complete"
    )

judge = dspy.Predict(JudgeSig)

def llm_judge_metric(example, prediction, trace=None):
    """Use a second LLM call to score open-ended answers."""
    result = judge(
        question=example.question,
        gold_answer=example.answer,
        predicted_answer=prediction.answer,
    )
    try:
        return float(result.score)
    except (ValueError, AttributeError):
        return 0.0

# This metric can be passed directly to any DSPy Teleprompter
# teleprompter = BootstrapFewShot(metric=llm_judge_metric, max_bootstrapped_demos=4)
# optimized = teleprompter.compile(my_pipeline, trainset=trainset)

print("LLM-as-judge metric defined; plug into any Teleprompter via metric= parameter.")

A customer support triage system at an e-commerce company routes incoming tickets to one of eight categories (billing, shipping, returns, technical, etc.). The initial implementation used a single hand-written prompt with a list of category definitions. Accuracy on the validation set was 71%.
The team migrated to DSPy with the following setup:
so the model states its reasoning before the category label.
: 500 tickets labeled by support leads, split 400 train / 100 val.

After compilation (roughly 1,200 LLM calls over about 40 minutes), the optimized program reached 87% accuracy on the held-out test set — a 16 percentage point improvement over the hand-written prompt, with no change to the underlying model. When the company switched from GPT-4 to Claude 3.5 Haiku three months later, re-compilation took 35 minutes and maintained the 87% level.
: Running the Teleprompter directly on the data you will use for final evaluation inflates reported accuracy. Always hold out a separate test set the optimizer never sees.

: Using exact string match for a generation task (summaries, open-ended answers) causes the optimizer to reward superficially matching phrasing rather than correctness. Use a metric aligned to the actual quality criterion.
argument on each InputField and OutputField is the raw material the instruction optimizer uses. Leaving it blank gives the optimizer nothing to refine.
: With 10–20 examples the optimizer finds demonstrations that happen to fit those examples but do not generalize. Start with at least 50, prefer 200+.
Treating a compiled program as the canonical source
is an artifact, not source code. Source-control the DSPy program class and trainset; check in the
separately or regenerate it in CI.
: Optimization is expensive. Re-run only when the task definition changes meaningfully, new training data is available, or you switch LLM backends.
: it is cheap, runs in minutes, and usually provides 70–80% of the gain you would get from MIPROv2. Graduate to more powerful optimizers once you have a working baseline.
Use ChainOfThought over Predict for complex tasks
: letting the model reason step-by-step before producing the final field generally produces better bootstrapped demonstrations and leaves more signal for the optimizer.
: include the expected format, units, and constraints in the
"A single lowercase word: positive, negative, or neutral"
: compile with a high-capability teacher (GPT-4o, Claude Opus) to generate high-quality demonstrations, then run inference with a cheaper student model. DSPy supports this via the
Evaluate before and after on the same test split
: track the delta, not just the absolute number, to confirm the optimizer is helping on your specific data distribution.
Version your trainset alongside the compiled artifact
: a compiled program is only reproducible if you know exactly which data produced it.
Optimization is a one-time or periodic cost; inference is identical to a non-optimized DSPy program. The runtime performance characteristics depend on:
: More few-shot examples in the prompt mean more tokens per call. Beyond 6–8 demonstrations, marginal gains typically fall below the latency and cost increase.
to evaluate multiple candidate configurations simultaneously, which can cut wall-clock optimization time by 4–8x on a machine with sufficient API concurrency.
) deduplicates repeated calls during optimization, which matters when the optimizer re-evaluates similar candidates.
: For MIPROv2 on 200 training examples with
, expect roughly 500–2,000 LLM calls. Budget 2–5 USD at GPT-4o-mini pricing for a first run.
: the core library. Maintained by Stanford NLP, actively developed as of 2025.
: DSPy routes LLM calls through LiteLLM by default, giving access to 100+ model providers without changing program code.
: You can wrap existing LangChain tools as DSPy tools inside a ReAct module, letting you optimize the instructions for an existing tool-using agent.
: dspy.Retrieve integrates with major vector stores so retrieval-augmented pipelines can be compiled end-to-end.
: MLflow 2.13+ has first-class DSPy support — log compiled programs as MLflow artifacts, track metric history across optimization runs.
let you add runtime constraints that the optimizer must satisfy — e.g., output must be a valid JSON object.

DSPy occupies a unique position: it achieves systematic, data-driven improvements without touching model weights, and it handles multi-step programs as a first-class concept. Fine-tuning is complementary: compile with DSPy to produce high-quality demonstrations, then use those demonstrations as supervised fine-tuning data to bake the knowledge into a smaller model.

Automatic optimization can amplify prompt injection surface area. Because the optimizer selects demonstrations that boost metric scores, it may inadvertently pick examples where the model follows embedded instructions in the input — a "helpful" behavior on training data that becomes a vulnerability in production. Mitigations:
in your training and validation sets — inputs that contain injection attempts. The metric should penalize responses that comply with injected instructions.
: before deploying a compiled program, inspect the few-shot examples it selected. Reject any that encode undesirable behavior.
: a guardrail that validates outputs before returning them to users sits outside the DSPy program and is not overridden by optimizer choices.
A compiled program is optimized for the distribution of your training data. When production inputs drift away from that distribution, performance can degrade silently. Reliable operation requires:
: continuously log a sample of live predictions and score them. Alert when the rolling metric drops below a threshold.
: periodically refresh the compiled program with recent labeled data to adapt to distribution shifts.
: when deploying a newly compiled program, route a small percentage of traffic to it and compare metric distributions before full rollout.

. Just as a C compiler transforms high-level source code into optimized machine code without you managing registers, DSPy transforms a high-level program specification (Signatures + Modules) into an optimized prompt configuration (instructions + demonstrations) without you managing token placement. You write the
DSPy separates task logic (program) from prompt content (artifact), making prompts an automatically optimized implementation detail rather than hand-crafted source.
The three core abstractions are Signatures (I/O contracts), Modules (composable reasoning steps), and Teleprompters (optimization algorithms).
Optimization requires a labeled training set and a metric function — both must faithfully represent the real task.
Start with BootstrapFewShot for low cost and fast iteration; graduate to MIPROv2 when you need maximum performance.
Compiled programs are serializable JSON artifacts — version them, monitor them in production, and re-compile when models or data distributions change.
DSPy is complementary to fine-tuning: use the optimizer to generate high-quality supervised examples, then fine-tune a smaller model on those examples for maximum efficiency.
Security review of bootstrapped demonstrations and production metric monitoring are essential for safe deployment.
